old_claim.note: Electric Power Meters provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Chemical process control is an application of process-control instrumentation: measurements, controllers, recorders, control valves, feedback loops, and sampling practices are the control substrate.

source_shape.source_locator: Historical Perspective and Overview sections describe 1920s-1930s automatic feedback control for process variables, pneumatic instruments/controllers/recorders, central control rooms, instrumentation, control valves, sampling, and feedback loops in chemical and petrochemical plants.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes the instrumentation and feedback-control architecture used in chemical and petrochemical process control.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Process_control_instrumentation is the direct substrate for the node, while electric power meters are only a neighboring measurement technology and not the chemical-process-control mechanism.

ontology_before: Chemical Process Control depended on electric_power_meters, implying utility metering was the enabling measurement substrate.
ontology_after: Chemical Process Control depends on process_control_instrumentation as the direct instrumentation, control-valve, recorder, and feedback-loop substrate.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: chemical_process_control now requires process_control_instrumentation and no longer directly depends on electric_power_meters.
would_reject_if: The chemical_process_control node is narrowed to electric-utility metering rather than chemical and petrochemical plant control.
A source shows electric power meters are required for the scoped chemical-process control practice.
The process_control_instrumentation node is narrowed away from gauges, valves, controllers, recorders, and feedback practices.
short_reason: Process-control instrumentation is the direct sourced prerequisite; electric power meters were too indirect.
